commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Human review is needed because the remaining blockers are integrated contributor proof and maintainer policy acceptance, not a narrow code repair.; Needs attention: No supply-chain issue was found, but the diff intentionally changes a security-sensitive Codex MCP exposure boundary that needs maintainer acceptance. (sha=43500c410c0ab3e288a4736270a6671675efc5c5)
commands.review_summary:
The PR decouples Codex user MCP server attachment from native code-mode gating, filters user MCP projection by existing tool allowlist entries, emits Codex `enabled_tools` for exact server-tool grants, and updates docs/tests.

PR surface: Source +134, Tests +166, Docs +28. Total +328 across 8 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a feature PR. Source inspection confirms current main's coupled native/user-MCP gate and the PR's new allowlist-aware projection, but I did not run an integrated runtime repro.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review is needed because the remaining blockers are integrated contributor proof and maintainer policy acceptance, not a narrow code repair.

target.title: feat(codex): scope user MCP server attachment by the tool allowlist
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

An OpenClaw operator running a **Codex-runtime agent** cannot give that agent access to a configured MCP server without also granting it Codex's full native code-mode shell/filesystem surface. The two are conflated behind a single gate.

In `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`, `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` returns true only when the effective tool allowlist contains a literal `*`. That one boolean (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled`) is then reused, unchanged, in `attempt-startup.ts` as `userMcpServersEnabled` â€” which decides whether `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projects `cfg.mcp.servers` into the Codex thread at all. Consequences:

1. **All-or-nothing.** Any least-privilege allowlist (`tools.allow` / `tools.alsoAllow` without `*`) silently disables **every** user MCP server, even ones the allowlist explicitly names. The MCP process spawns but its tools never register in the compiled toolset.
2. **Over-exposure when servers do attach.** `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projected **every** enabled `cfg.mcp.servers` entry, filtered only by the per-server `codex.agents` field â€” it never consulted the tool allowlist.

This is a general gap for any scoped Codex agent with any MCP server, not a single integration.

## What Changed

Decouple the two capabilities and make MCP attachment flow through the **same allowlist contract OpenClaw already uses for every other tool** (`tool-policy.ts`):

- **Native code mode is unchanged** â€” `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` keeps its fail-closed, wildcard-only rule.
- **`shouldEnableUserMcpServersForCodexAppServer`** now answers only *"is it safe to attach external MCP servers this turn?"* (not a memory-flush run; OpenClaw sandbox, if active, can be honored). The per-server decision moves to the projection.
- **`resolveCodexMcpServerAllow`** matches against the **provider-safe model-facing tool ids** OpenClaw exposes â€” `<sanitizedServer>__<sanitizedTool>` via `sanitizeServerName` + `normalizeToolName`, **not** the raw `mcp.servers` key â€” and honors the same entries as the bundle-MCP allowlist contract:
  - `undefined` â†’ include, all tools (legacy unrestricted behavior preserved)
  - `*`, `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins` â†’ include, all tools
  - `<server>__*` â†’ include that server, all tools
  - `<server>__<tool>` â†’ include that server, scoped to the named tool(s)
  - no matching token â†’ exclude the server
- **`buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch`** projects **only** referenced servers, and for exact-tool entries emits Codex's per-server **`enabled_tools`** filter so other tools from an attached server stay unreachable. The thread-config fingerprint varies with the patch, so a thread rebinds when scope changes.

### Addresses ClawSweeper review
- **Provider-safe matching** (was raw-key): `Outlook Graph` (ids `outlook-graph__*`) resolves; `bundle-mcp` / `group:plugins` honored.
- **Per-tool scope** (was discarded): exact entries emit `enabled_tools` instead of attaching the whole server. (`enabled_tools` carries the sanitized tool fragment; exact for provider-safe names, fail-closed otherwise, since the live tool list isn't known at projection time.)

## Files
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/{dynamic-tool-build,run-attempt,attempt-startup,thread-lifecycle}.ts` â€” decouple + plumb `userMcpServersEnabled`, pass `toolsAllow` to the projection
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.ts` â€” `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow` + allowlist-aware projection with `enabled_tools`
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`; tests as below

## Evidence

### Unit tests (`vitest run`)
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts` â€” **24 passed** (undefined/`*`/`bundle-mcp`/`group:plugins` attach all; `opik__*` only opik, no `enabled_tools`; exact `opik__list,opik__read` â†’ `enabled_tools:["list","read"]`; provider-safe `Outlook Graph` â†” `outlook-graph__*`; `["message"]`/`[]` â†’ none).
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` â€” **51 passed**, incl. the assertion that `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface(["opik__*"]) === false` â€” **native code mode stays disabled** under a scoped MCP allowlist.

### Runtime proof â€” patched OpenClaw projection (real code, unmocked)
Driving the actual `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` over a config with `opik` + `notion` + `atlas`:
```
toolsAllow=undefined                    -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["*"]                        -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["opik__*"]                  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["opik__list","opik__read"]  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":["list","read"]}
toolsAllow=["notion__*"]                -> servers=["notion"]                  scope={"notion":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["message","web_search"]     -> servers=[]                          scope=none
```

### Runtime proof â€” real Codex app-server (`codex-cli 0.139.0`)
Drove the **real** `codex app-server` over JSON-RPC stdio with two stub stdio MCP servers (each exposing `list,read,write,schema`): `opik` configured with `enabled_tools=["list"]` (what the projection emits for `["opik__list"]`), `notion` unfiltered (the `["notion__*"]` case). Then `mcpServerStatus/list` (`detail: toolsAndAuthOnly`):
```
=== LIVE MCP TOOLS REGISTERED BY CODEX ===
opik:   [list]                       <- enabled_tools filter honored by the real binary
notion: [list, read, schema, write]  <- unscoped server, all tools
(other entries omitted: unrelated servers from local ~/.codex/config.toml)
```
This confirms the real Codex binary (a) treats `enabled_tools`/`disabled_tools` as first-class per-server fields (visible in `codex mcp get` schema output) and (b) registers **only** the scoped tool when the filter is set.

### Types
Full-CI `check-prod-types` / `check-lint` / `check-docs` pass; standalone extension `tsc` shows only pre-existing plugin-sdk `dist`-sync noise, none referencing this PR's symbols.

## Security posture
- Native code-mode shell/file access still requires a literal `*` â€” unchanged (proven above).
- Default-open for unrestricted (`undefined`) agents preserved; sandbox-without-exec-server still attaches no user MCP servers.
- Net effect is *tighter*: scoped allowlists attach only referenced servers; exact-tool entries attach only named tools via `enabled_tools`.

đź¤– Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
